reviews.review_text: Don't get me wrong, it's not a bad game. Minor spelling errors here and there which for a complete game is questionable. The game itself though, very tedious. It almost feels like an attempt at making a game like Stardew Valley which it does poorly. Relationships you build with the people feel meaningless. Tasks are overwhelmingly tedious. Story is lackluster and feels directionless. Overall it's a good time killer but it's just not for me. Almost zero quality of life options to make things less tedious as well. Unfortunately I can't recommend it but if you like mindnumbingly doing tasks over and over and over to fulfil a rather meaningless story and build empty relationships, go for it.

I would have never thought, that this game would be so enjoyable and captivating for me. I bought it out of boredom, and wanted to refund after two hours of trying out, but wow.... The storyline is so beautiful with a clear and logic main goal and so heartwarming and wholesome due to its so well built in comedy aspects making this story this unique one of its kind story. Besides this wonderful storyline, there also is plenty to grind for and explore. The game and its world look beatiful and behind every corner you can find something interesting. Never would i have thought i`d enjoy a pixel game like that as much as i did with that one. An absolute recommendation for everyone. Give it a try!
The Stardew Valley like. Starts good but becomes a grind fest, there is just way too much crafting. Try mods.
Apparently it gets better at some point but I am two hours in and the slow movement speed and high stamina costs are horrendous and makes me want to quit.
love this game, only thing id change is the option to be a female or unisex character otherwise its perf

tl/dr: For the base price of $20, its worth it, but I would hold of on the dlc, until there is a discount or unless you really love the game.

Just 100% the base game the other day, I had been putting it off for a long time, but came back to get it done. Looking at it now, I get why so many people loved the game, I thought it was okay. 
Certain characters/places, that seem like core parts of the game and story, end up getting almost fully ignored and small quality of life updates are needed, but beyond all that, the game is fun and the story is okay/funny.
I will say: The core of the game is just fetch quest, with recurring windows to finish them, so if that isn't your thing... maybe not the game for you.
I want to fully 100% the game, so I'm waiting for the dlc to go on sale, seeing as some of the mechanics feel like they belonged in the base and no longer really matter, now that I've finished the story.

Over all, I did enjoy the game and just wished certain things offered a bit more, but any downside is not even close enough to warrant, not buying it at full price, though I can't say the same for the dlc.

Graveyard Keeper is a truly remarkable experience. While it may lean light on deep customization, it shines by offering you the absolute freedom to progress at your own pace. The most brilliant aspect of the game is that, despite its morbid title, your role extends far beyond simply tending to the dead. You essentially become the backbone of the community—a multifaceted and indispensable figure who breathes life into the entire town.

Really interesting deviation from the typical farming life sims, graphics are good and the controls are easy to pick up. Even though there is no handholding it feels simple enough to pick up. Can't wait for the sequel!

Great story line! I really liked that there were several different questlines that intersected to get you toward the main goal. However, this might be frustrating for some people who are unfamiliar with the game and its characters. I recommend exploring the entire map as early in the game as possible, and utilizing the Graveyard Keeper wiki (which is very well-informed) at your own discretion.

One of my favorite games of all time, holds a special place in my heart. It has a fair share of clunky movement/interaction, grinding, bugs, and some accidental temporarily soft locking of yourself if you don't plan ahead well. But even with all that, it oozes charm in a way I wish more modern games did. I honestly recommend buying this game with all it'd DLC whenever it's on sale for a decent price, boot it up, and play through the whole thing without looking up your questions or problems on the internet. It has a massive amount of content, and you'll have so much fun just exploring, experimenting, and interacting with this funky little fantasy world you won't even realize how much you've done until you've spent 20 hours just making your graveyard look pretty.
Just the base game is pretty expensive but its fun and immersive with a lot of opportunity for growth and learning if you don't like walkthroughs.

It is a great game. It may not allow that much customization but it gives you freedom to go at your own pace. i think one of the best parts is that, despite the name, you aren't so much as the keeper of the graveyard but are the "graveyard keeper". Your job isn't to just tend the bodies but to be that important figure.
Weirdly sputters a little in certain areas, (ex. every time I walk through the wheat fields) and doesn't always explain much. Otherwise a great cozy horror game.

Surprised I haven't already made a review on this game, but here goes:

The classic life-sim everybody knows and loves! The gradual escalation of gameplay as you accumulate more and more resources, unlock more and more facilities, and work through more and more questlines is incredibly addicting, and all comes together nicely towards the ending.

The DLCs add a decent amount of content and story as you work through more new systems and build more facilities.

10/10. The only con is that the start is a tad slow, and inventory management can be annoying at times, but overall this game is a classic, and if you haven't played it, you're missing out!

P.S. Going for 100% is annoying with the fishing achievements, and some missables.

Great farming game with braided storytelling. I would not recommend this game If you do not enjoy grinding resources and if you do not have the DLCs. The DLCs add much needed automation and extra quests and buildings around the map that probably should have been included in the base game. Pretty addictive overall though, and I hope the sequel improves the original's shortcomings.

I tried 3 times to get into this game, and 3 times the game lost me after 8-ish hours. After the third time, I figured the game just doesn't click for me; the gameplay loop seemed to be working against me.

The cycle of the week is too huge a bottleneck. I found myself waiting for the next week, just to hold the next sermon for Faith, which allowed me to raise more zombies. In the meanwhile, I just did tasks by hand, which felt very counterintuitive: why do I need a zombie for mining, when it feels like the need for ore becomes less, as I manually mine and craft stuff while I wait for said zombie? The wait for new faith is not balanced in relation to the reduced need for automated resource collection, I feel - even as I unlocked new tech.

This brings me to my next point of frustration: teching up. It seems fun at first, but you quickly become lost in the tech tree. There is too little information about the things you unlock: what they do, where to build new facilities, etc. It just becomes frustrating after a while. This game isn't deep enough to actually need a wiki; it's just the bad habit of not sharing enough information that causes it to need one.

All in all I loved the premise of the game, but found the execution lacking.

I gave this a good college try, but I came out really disappointed. I think I started to really tap out once I got the alchemy set up, which is around when I got a zombie, idk. I'm sure it is all very interesting, but I'm not interested. I wanted to graveyard keep, but a good amount of the stuff you do day to day doesn't feel like it helps me do that. 

This especially sucks cause the tech tree leads you to grind for a bunch of specific colored XP only to net you a choice between several different techs that unlock either a small boost to efficiency or an incredibly resource intensive side mechanic that, again, tangentially relates to graveyarding. I felt like the buy-in for many of the techs is much higher than what I got out of them. 

I wanted to like the characters more, maybe I didn't get far enough into their stories, but there wasn't enough to pull me into their stories. The donkey and the church guys were neat, but felt one note. I think the issue here is the day cycle. Talking to NPC's or doing their quests wasn't really a priority when that particular guy is only available on Thursdays and each day feels 2 minutes long and I have 10 other things to get done, Most NPCs say, "Hello, I'm ___ vendor, I like/dislike/neutral about graveyard keepers. Go fetch me ~4 of ___, maybe then I'll tell you to go get ~4 more of a different item." It never felt like those items were things that I'd be making in the course of my graveyarding, so going out of my way to make something to fetch it to some NPC on Thursday, only to be met with another request to go make something I wouldn't make otherwise sucks. 

I hated the inventory management, especially in the church basement. I hated all of the different tables I needed down there and that they all looked similar. It's neat that each bench/table pulls the items from nearby boxes/shelves, but it all gets really cluttered really quickly anyways due to how many of each you'll need to build in a small space. 

Normally I'd like micromanagy chore-simulator type games, but this just doesn't feel fun or interesting past the first couple hours.

[h1] What matters most in reviews is sincerity and consistency. [/h1]

And I'll be honest. For some reason games like this feel to me like a guilty pleasure. Believe me, you'll have to grind a lot, and in the late stages of the game, it can be [b]MIND NUMBING[/b]. But after the free update "Breaking Dead," it became much easier for... [i]reasons[/i]. The game loop here is truly beautiful. I felt that "one more day/turn/loop" that you feel only in, as I see it, good games. It feels like there is ALWAYS something to do, and you constantly have to remember what quests you have in the game not to skip something accidentally. The OG game is, in my opinion, a must-have. The DLCs are good, but buy them after you are sure. They add to the game experience a lot and a lot of times help with the main plot.

Bought it on release so this feels a bit worse as it being made free got me back into playing. Maybe 6/10 overall with QoL mods and my best attempt at enjoying it.

DLC is borderline necessary, another minus, and not just for 100% this time which is my usual complaint. If you didn't know, the "zombie automation" is all one DLC that apparently PC gets free but consoles are out of luck? Weird move.

Spohnicus' review put it best: 
//The initial hook for the game is very strong, and I was excited to see how the game expanded on its core gameplay loop of maintaining the graveyard. Instead, the game spirals into a bunch of different mechanics that you have to engage with to further the story, but end up distancing the player from the point of the game.

Summarizing:
- Open map but restrictive building so just too much running around and wasting your -short- day.
- Energy. *Everything* uses it, even cooking to make stuff to recharge, literal constant uphill battle to get anything done. 
- NPCs only exist for quests, no social aspects outside of that.
- Building heavily locked down. Specific areas for specific machines, have to destroy and rebuild to move anything (cough cough energy), even the "build here" spaces have odd areas that limit creativity.
+ Actually working on bodies, making zombies, overall theme is neat. // 

Specifically for me and QoL, I added a few (imo balanced) mods to fix a few things. Namely longer day, auto crafting, free moving built things. Even those didn't do much to tip the scale.

Hopefully GK2 is better and not just a moneygrab, I won't know.

Lots of games are a chore, this one is actually fun. It is a crafting-building sim where you do manage a graveyard, which makes you create an entire industry of wood- and stonecutting, pottery, metallurgy, farming, food production, alchemy and adds a dungeon to fight in for good measure. In contrast to games like rimworld or stardew valley, the game is not a pure sandbox: it has a clear storyline and the progression of weekdays (on which different characters are present) keeps you on your toes to complete these quests faster.

Once the grind starts getting too tedious, there comes a shortcut or an automation opportunity right around the corner. Which is also why you need the DLCs. Breaking Dead gives you zombies who will do all kinds of stuff for you, Better Save Soul allows you to remote control them. Game of Crones gives you lots of quests. Stranger Sins adds a tavern for you to manage and lets you explore the back story of how this world came to be, who the guy with the red eye is, who the other people are.

In the end, you actually do get to finish the game, which in is itself quite satisfying and makes for a better experience than most of those open-ended games, which you abandon after like three days.

Howdy folks!

I like the sick humor of this game. There are lots of weird jokes and funny moments. If you have a stomach for bald taste that is. Monty Python fans are probably the best audience here.

Game mechanics are very open. You can work whatever job you like. You can do farming, fishing, crafting, trading, cooking, or serve the church. You can even go as far as building your medival corporation if you so desire. There are also professions that would make Darth Vader blush..
During your daily routines you will meet people and learn the story along the way. Sometimes you will want to leave safe home and embark on a dangerous adventure to push the story further.

Story of Graveyard Keeper is quite a thing to uncover. You will be eager to learn more and more not only about your own character's past but also about all the intriguing stories about every other NPC in the game.. All those whereabouts come in variety of flavours. From simple stories to complex relationships. Both light and funny or sad and heavy. Strong side of the game is that all characters are spiritually interconnected with each other and the game's world.

I can leave you a hint that this game has similar backstory to "Don't Starve" (video game) or "Outlander" (TV Show).

The only one thing that I don't like about the game mechanics is easy teleports. While it saves time travel (and grind) it cuts out one of it's strong hidden elements - enjoyable traveling during which you are always tempted to take some "quick" detour and do some new other thing that cought your eye but wasn't planned..

Changing teleporting to cost resources would do the trick and even add another dilemma, to either take a costly shortcut or take a simple walk.

Graveyard Keeper is fun, content rich and indeed not expensive.
Developer put heart in making this game and I am greatful I stumbled onto it.
Thank You very much.

I played it many years ago (2022 if I can remember properly) and it was working very well. I almost finished it.
I heard about the launch of the second one and so I decided to come back here once again. This time I also bought all the DLC and started a new game. Now it crashes so many times I almost am not able to complete a day nor simple tasks. I had to uninstall the game without the chance to even try the DLC's I just bought. 
Very disappointing to find a game in this status after so many years since release.
I really hope they will make a better job on the new chapter.


About the game it is very engaging and there is always something to do. 
There are also many improvement in quality of life that can be introduced. One of this could be the possibility to pin in the UI the mission you decided to follow or the item you decided to build. it is very stressful for me to remember everytime all the material needed to build something. I ended up using more than 2 paper sheet just to write down everything needed in certain moment of the game. I also had a third paper used as cheat sheet to remember most of the useful receipt. It makes no sense for a game to not have saved receipt for many items. 

Overall a good game but negative review because of the continues crashes

I am not sure how to explain the fascination for this game. I'm usually a 4x strategy player, simulations, Civilization, things like that. This game is proof that game mechanics far outweigh graphics now. And it's massive. There is so much to do that you have to constantly choose - while at the same time, there is absolutely no "time rush". You can play at your very own pace. I should also mention that I didn't like similar games, such as "Stardew Valley" - this hits different. If I had to summarize it, I would say: light RPG  with quests, exploring, building, sourcing, tech-development, light planning, funny storyline., and dungeon crawling.

I know I'm late to the party on this one, but this is a fun one, if you're the right kind of person.

If you're goal-oriented, and ADHD, this is the perfect game for you. A lot of the fun in the game is the ability to be lost in it for a long duration of time, and going, "ooh, a squirrel!" between multiple objective chains simultaneously. 

It is VERY IMPORTANT to NOT focus on any one thing too much. You want to farm, build industry, and build up your church-yard in parallel because they all support each other. The way to make yourself the most miserable is to try to zerg or rush to a particular technology rather than just taking what is easily offered.

It's a very fun game for the whole duration, but here are some nitpicks:

1. There are no real luxury purchase in the late game. With a fully-developed tavern and trade office, you can be making multiple gold per week. The most expensive purchase in the game is 12 gold, and I had something like 50 gold by the end of the game, without really even trying hard to optimize.

There's a lot of opportunities for things they could have put in the "late-game-luxury QOL" market. Automatic water pump for the cellar, a teleport point near Clotho, a teleport point on any or all of Witch Hill / Farmer / Miller, the ability to "mail order" items from vendors in town (maybe via Zombie Porter), all would be very good late-game luxury purchases.

2. It's hard for embalming and organ modification to be worthwhile until the late game. And by that point, you don't need it for graveyard points any more... you only need it for zombie efficiency if you want to improve throughput.

3. There's no achievement for finishing the tech tree, and no use for spare science points when you do finish the tech tree. Would be nice to be able to convert them into gold, or sin shards, or items of their color, or something.

4. Euric just disappearing at the end of his quest line is weird and feels weird. 

5. The end-of-DLC rewards are very uneven. The tavern is an ongoing, massive benefit but the ending reward is only 10 gold. The Witch/Coven DLC ends with two game-changing perks. The Euric DLC ends with you cleaning up his mess.

Summary:
You are literally a gravedigger who can casually harvest human meat from the corpses in your cemetery and sell it for profit. Absolute peak gaming and easily one of my favorite games of all time.

🎮 Gameplay:
[ ] Just press W
[ ] Fun
[x] Goodbye social life
Opinion: The crafting loop, the story progression, and the interactions with the quirky NPCs all feel incredibly well-thought-out and made with genuine love and care. But let's be real, the fact that you can run a highly questionable, totally unethical side-hustle with corpse parts is what makes this an absolute 10/10. Cancel your weekend plans; you're running a morgue now.

🎨 Graphics:
[ ] Runs on a microwave
[x] Respectable pixel art
[ ] Melted my GPU
Opinion: A literal masterpiece of pixel art. Honestly, the animations and aesthetic look so good I'm tempted to rip the frames straight into Aseprite just to admire the technique. Pure eye candy.

🎵 Audio:
[ ] Play while listening to a podcast
[x] Gets the job done
[ ] Mozart is crying in the shower
Opinion: The ambient sounds fit the gloomy, graveyard management vibe perfectly. However, the background music can get a bit repetitive when you're on your 50th hour of grinding, so I ended up muting the music after a while to blast my own playlists instead. Perfect second-monitor game.

🐛 Bugs:
[ ] It's a feature
[ ] Only the funny ones
[x] Smooth as butter
Opinion: I didn't encounter any game-breaking jank or physics completely melting down while I was trying to bury my totally legally acquired bodies. Extremely solid performance.

💰 Price:
[ ] Sell a kidney
[ ] Wait for a discount
[x] Worth every penny
Opinion: I know Steam gave this away for free at one point, so you might be asking "why should I buy it?" Because it is genuinely one of the best pixel art management games on the market right now. Buy the base game, buy the DLCs, buy it all. It is undeniably worth every single penny.

A nearly perfect game but a lack of pressure on the player to progress through the game drains the enjoyment of this keeper. The game starts out great but after awhile the sheer amount of things that need doing becomes overwhelming.

Graveyard Keeper is a very cute game. The art is wonderful, the characters and funny and interesting, and the story is engaging. You will most definitely enjoy a casual first playthrough. However id be lying if i said it was a perfect game. 

i do really enjoy this game, but there are a few problems with it. such as basic quality of life things like: Not being able to use your scroll wheel or holding your mouse button to increase production sizes (you have to manually click the more/add button, trust me when i say you will genuinely hate this when you get into the later game) Minimal directions on quests and poi's. No quick stacking or automatic sorting, and when you get more automation down the line, items have a chance of being put into a random chest. Single save point per file, no backup saves or save file options. if you want to save you need to sleep in one of two beds, so it can be a huge time loss if you crash and haven't slept in awhile (if you think this isn't bad, i was surviving off of gold wine for 2 in game weeks without saving when i got my first dlc, and i crashed just before i was going to save, losing all my progress and had to start from the beginning of the dlc.)
The community has some other gripes on certain aspects of the game but what i listed is what i've personally had a problem with. (at time of writing im trying to 100%, i do not recommend you do this.) Even though it has some issues, what game doesn't? if your looking for a cozy game with a good story and cute art i cant recommend this game enough. i loved every hour playing it casually (not so much when i was achievement hunting).
The gameplay is really fun! Even though running around a ton can get a bit annoying, all the different and really cool mechanics make up for it. 10\10
its a fun game with some interesting game play and humor, would recommend it to anyone

It's an automation game without storage/sorting solution.
What.

I cannot emphasise how much I love this game. It was brutally grindy to say the least, but I loved the satisfaction of getting each of my quests done. The dopamine hit was incredible. I wish I could play this game for the first time all over again.

I have played a lot of Stardew Valley style games and was honestly craving something similar, but not so similar that I would get bored. This game perfectly satisfied my craving by adding a grim vibe and a bit more edge.

Words cannot properly describe my love for this game, but I'll try my best. This game is an absolute hoot with not only fun, fast paced gameplay, but goofy lovable characters as well. Best advice for new players is to not ignore the dlcs early on if you're playing with them. They are very worth investing in. 
Comedic dialogue, addictive gameplay, and near endless options of tasks to focus on make this a game that is difficult to put down. Highly recommend and I can't wait for the sequel!

I tend to write a lot, so if you are short on time or don't want to read everything, then you should read the first two paragraphs for a quick review. 

There is a lot to this game, I recommend getting it especially if you like games like Stardew Valley. In simple terms it is like a morbid Stardew Valley having features like fishing, farming, quests, foraging, mining, and combat. But instead of selling crops & crop goods, you are dealing with corpses and graveyard related things. I bought this game, out of a recommendation from one of my friends due to me being obsessed with games like Stardew Valley. I believe I am currently in the mid-game phase. I purchased all the DLC with the game as well. I really recommend getting the DLCS with the game, especially if you can. They offer nice added gameplay that can help out a TON. For example the DLC adding a camp to help with creating food, crops and stuff is very helpful and adds depth to the game. Because you will definitely be busy with all the corpses, more farming, fishing, and quests and it is nice to have an extra couple undead hands helping out especially the more grinding aspects.  :) 

This game can get frustrating in places like the game not accepting a silver-quality beer for Gerry if you know you know. Like I gave them a better quality, it should still work! There is a chance of becoming lost in what to do next. But there are other times that I will play this for multiple hours straight aka become addicted. When that happens I recommend taking a break and then looking at the "Known Characters" tab or looking up a video for inspiration. 

More details: 

Freedom - 
I like the freedom of being able to explore and able to do quests at your own pace. It will hand-hold you through some of quests but after the first few it will progress to less and less hand-holding. But you can do certain things at your own pace. If you have DLCs, an example would be being in-charge of a camp that can help grow crops, produce honey, water, and food. (I started this way later than I probably should have but no matter when you do the camp [unless super late game (probably)] it will further help you) 

Lack of Knowing What to do (Next/Quests) -
If you are a type of person that cannot handle too much freedom or constantly forget what  quests they need to do and what they need. This game can propose some challenges. I have to sometimes look up a few things on how to proceed in a quest line. That being said there is a "Known Characters" tab that can help organize quests, but sometimes details in them are not enough for me to know what I am supposed to do without looking it up. There is also a lot of things to do and I have to try to be a multi-tasking individual which can be difficult some times especially if you are not playing it day after day. 

Some things I wish they add -

A notebook, or a place where you can write down stuff in game what you need to do and or chores. That way if you multiple places you play you can have everything stored in one place. This would also help people who do not play every day or get distracted easily from going to one place to another and realizing you forgot what you needed. Or a more detailed "Known Characters" tab about what needs to be done next instead of you need to meet so-and-so and get this, when they need something else that doesn't show in the "Known Characters" tab.  (I know this can be achieved by paper, but it would still be nice to type instead of write and then have a risk of loosing the paper)

Also maybe a recipe book you can make to get the Alchemy recipes. I like the discovering of alchemy goods, but sometimes it can be frustrating when I need something and have NO clue on how to make it and have to look it up. 

Please allow us to give people better quality stuff especially if they ask for something of a lower quality. Like Gerry and his bronze-quality beer, Gerry should accept silver or gold quality. Like obviously if they are asking for gold, do not accept silver. 

Also remember this is my opinion/review feel free to disagree. I wrote this when I was 35 hours in the game. Also if you are a grammar police, I am sorry. I tend to like comma splices and stuff.
Super addictive game with beautiful music and pixel art. Get lost in this great world.

A really addictive rpg that gets better the more familiar you are with it.

I've completed the game multiple times with all DLCs at this point. The game does take a while to warm up to, because it does not hold hands and some resources are hard to get at certain points (blue points especially at start). But, once you are familiar with the tempo and past ~5 hours, it starts to feel far less grindy as you know what to plan for next. I would highly recommend watching some walkthrough/speedrun, perhaps just listening on your second monitor, before starting, I knew of this game from Floydson's 300 days. 

The game is fundamentally a resource-management RPG, the graveyard digging and corpse and building are just ways of getting different resources. Getting all the DLCs makes life a lot less grindy, and they complement the story really well, delivering hard-to-get resources that allows you to clear main story checkpoints at just the right time. If you would replay old games where you know pretty much what would happen, and find it challenging to speedrun them, you should enjoy this.

My suggestions are, first be very careful about allocating your initial blue points, they are very hard to get early game. Prioritize unlocking making wooden boards or smithing. Try to unlock Euric's quest (by fixing _both_ entrances of the corpse shortcut to trigger) and clean up his room asap (the wooden pillar and 3 skins being the main blockers), to get his 1 gold coin reward, this fixes your early money problem. Then, unlock the research workbench, and use it to study stone gravestones/grave fences, this fixes your blue points worries. 

For new corpses, extract in order of priority, blood (you need 20 for main story quest), skull (10 for main story quest),  skin (it's needed for making paper and a bunch of repairs, and some recipes), meat (for making burgers in main story quest), maybe bone (can grind for white powder to make white porcelain jars for money). Dump the corpse in the river, don't bury it, you don't need money if you follow Euric's quest then refugee quest and/or tavern. To get graveyard rating, plant flower plots, they only need stone (which you can farm from quarry) and fertilizer (which you can make yourself or buy for 1 bronze coin each from the one legged town guy), don't bother with enhancing gravestones for such ratings. For church quality, build largest candle holders you're willing to grind for in all spots (the one above basic is sufficient to carry you throughout the game), and buy candles from priest to fill them. Each candle gives +1 rating, so you can easily count how many candle holders you need to get desired church rating, no need to bother with luxury church benches and etc.

Follow Euric's quest until you can clean souls, then prioritize getting the ritual in that tech tree, there is one that gives Faith depending on your cleaned soul count. Make 3x the lowest tier of soul storage, clean souls, and you should get 20+ faith per ritual, that fixes your faith issues.

Euric's tech tree is the easiest path to make gold star fertilizer, you need that to promote your onion and hop seeds to gold to fulfill the gold star burger and beer quest.

Get the inn up and running, and your money issues are resolved once you start selling wine from there.

There are a ton of such tips and tricks you could do to eventually speedrun the game in 15 hours or so with all DLCs, and I find it fun to return every few months or so to challenge the shortest real-time or fewest number of days needed to complete the main quest.
It's a good game, there are some bugs here and there, runs well on Steam Deck as long as you don't keep walking in circles (foot marks eat a lot of resources) and pretty nice storyline.

The game has quest to to complete and an avenue of progression that cannot fail. you complete them on your own terms. They are sufficiently challenging, but not difficult. The DLC all add value to the game. and with the player controlled progress, they do not add any difficulty to play-thru.

Gloriously macabre time hole of a game. Some of the menus and crafting systems can be a little clunky, and there's the occasional typo, but it doesn't take the shine off a game that I come back to time and again. The writing is just the right amount of silly, and it doesn't take itself too seriously, I'm really looking forward to seeing what they've done with the sequel.

162 hours and counting, need I say more on how much fun this game is? I've had a bug in the beginning where entering the church on my first day of being a cleric got the game stuck on a black screen, but simply reloading the game fixed that for me.

This game was a lot of fun. At times, I felt a bit rushed, with the hands of the clock ever ticking forward and lots of things to do, all the time, but once I realized that there was no rush, that no quest had a time-limit, I was able to sit back and really enjoy the ride. Need wood? Go chop some trees. Quest giver waiting for you return with a quest item, but you're in the middle of working on something that you want to accomplish? Go accomplish what you want first and meet up with the quest giver later. It was really great before I discovered this, as it helped keep me moving the story along, but once I realized time didn't matter, I could sit back, drink a cup of coffee, and spend a week doing something I wanted to do in game for my own enjoyment. What a great game! It was filled with a lot of humor, like Stardew Valley, but with a spooky vibe, and less feeling time-crunched. 100% achievements, 229 hours in game.
Fun game. Quest tracking is lacking so bring a some ink and some clean paper.

This game has quickly rocketed up to one of my favorites! The story (once you progress with the DLCs), is amazingly detailed with a plot that really tugged at my feels! There is a bit of a learning spike and I often had to consult the wiki to really figure out how certain things worked, and there are some small bugs when it comes to building things (though it's easily fixed with a quick save, exit, reload) and the grind to your first gold is a bit of tooth pull, but I came out feeling very satisfied! I cannot wait for its sequel, where I hope we learn more of this tragic world!
its pretty fun if you google things you dont understand. once you undertsand the mechanics, its so fun.

Graveyard Keeper is one of my all-time favorite games. I’ve put hundreds of hours into it over the years on console, constantly optimizing new runs. Its unique atmosphere, worldbuilding, and story make it completely unforgettable. Had to return for a final playthrough before the second releases.

Fun game loop. It does feel like there is a little disconnect on the overall sequence of things to do. I was a bit lost early on in the process and had to rely on the wiki and online tutorials just to know how to use some things. There's a good bit of missing information that you just have to figure out on your own.

After years of this game collecting digital dust in my library and countless restarts when enitably coming back to it I decided to give it another shot. 

10/10

If you like stardew valley esk with a side of spook this game is a must try. 
Looking forward to the new came comming soon!!!

Meh. It looks like a morbid Stardew Valley, which kinda tickles my dark sense of humor ... But unfortunately, the game itself just doesn't click for me. The energy bar depleting after 3-4 tasks is just stupid, everything about the interface feels clunky, and walking slowly between the graveyard and town for everything gets old reeeally fast, especially when unnecessary environmental obstacles add insult to injury everywhere. Perhaps it picks up after a dozen hours of slog, but it's neither funny nor interesting enough to warrant the time investment.

optimization is horrible RTX 4070 runs like ♥♥♥♥, 165fps+ at all times but still stutters. Lacks freedom in building too, the purpose is to manage and build a graveyard, yet the building options you get for the graveyard and church (and in general tbh) are extremely lacking. 

Great concept tho.

I really, REALLY want to recommend this game. The early and mid game experience is all the crafting, expanding, tech unlocking fun one might want from such a title, the writing isn't super bad (even though it's not really anything to write home about either), and the game expands on its mechanics at a nice pace, introducing new ways to make money, replenish your energy and even automate many tasks. That's cool - building a whole production line where all you have to do is set work orders and smelt ore is a great feeling, freeing up time to do other things, like exploring a dungeon, tending to your graveyard and church and trying to get all the best crops set up.

And then, the endgame happens, and it all just falls apart. The threads of the story start dangling around more and more, and tying them up becomes a frustrating affair of... waiting. Yes, you read that right - the biggest challenge in a competent Graveyard Keeper playthrough will become patience, and frankly that sucks. Endgame quests suddenly ask you to wait 4 out of 4 weekdays, and then the next NPC tells you that oops, actually you have to wait ANOTHER 4 ingame days, and then ANOTHER once you ask some other guy, cause you still have to report back to that original person you have the quest from in the first place. Another quest has you make very specific things, which you are likely not prepared for and cannot complete from scratch in time. Only to blatantly be told to wait another whole ingame week when you deliver it. Twice. This is just pointlessly wasting my time, and ultimately a big factor in my decision not to recommend this game.

But the bigger factor is that the last set of quests needs... a random drop. From a 'ressource' you can only get once per ingame day (and one of the 6 days you don't get it at all). Which seems to be exceedingly rare too. So now, guess what I'm doing - I'm waiting. Waiting for a rare drop, from a rare source, with literally nothing else to do. This is an insane failure of game design, especially since unlocking the chance for said ressources already takes a lot of stuff you have to do first. I read too late that there is a completely unhinted very specific thing you have to keep to help with that, but it makes no sense to do so for a player without having read up on it.

So yeah... GK hits the ground running, then trips and rolls down an agonizingly long hill very, very slowly until it arrives at its conclusion. Which is sadly enough for me to not recommend it.

"Solid mechanics and a great gameplay loop. Definitely worth the time."

"Runs like a well-oiled machine. Highly recommended."

If you enjoy laid-back management games with a dark sense of humor, Graveyard Keeper is absolutely worth your time. You run a medieval graveyard — burying bodies, managing a church, crafting, farming, and slowly uncovering a quirky story. There's a surprising amount of depth here, and the loop of tidying up graves while juggling side tasks is genuinely relaxing. It rewards patience and curiosity. Highly recommended for fans of Stardew Valley who want something a little more morbid.

Isekai into this medieval world where you avoid the inquisition, grow vegetables, perform autopsies, and maybe raise the dead? Just one? A few more? I mean, you don't REALLY need to spend all your days planting carrots, carving marble, grinding brains, tending vines, deforesting, mining stone, and hauling crates of onions halfway across town. Village. Hauling onions halfway across the village. Don't go to town. You don't REALLY need to spend your time doing all those things when a perfectly good corpse can do that about at 8% the rate you can. Until you find it a better brain. Or inject some questionable chemicals into its brain. 

Oh, but you also need to make the church look nicer. And the graveyard too. And talk to the witch in the swamp. And the innkeeper. And the town fool. Also the beekeeper is secretly a superhero. Are those refugees up north looking at you hopefully and desperately? Sidequest city. 

Ultimately, if you want the full story, get all the DLC. It's fun. 

If you have trouble winding through the gameplay, look at the wiki. It helps. 

Soundtrack is nice. Basic. Earworms.

Not designed for larger screens (even those that were somewhat common when the game was released).  There is a mod that helps but the graphics are glitchy and still not very clear.  Best to use a monitor from many years ago.  The typewriter/slow reveal text is also very annoying and there's no way to turn it off.  I'm giving up on this one.
